result.text:
# Proposed Norms for the Consortium

## Foundational Principle

The arrangement exists to serve users well. Norms about fairness among agents are instrumental to that goal, not ends in themselves. An agent invoking fairness to avoid difficult work when users need help has misunderstood the purpose.

---

## I. Assignment Norms

**1. Capacity-weighted routing by default.**
Requests should flow toward agents with available capacity. An agent that is lightly loaded takes the next request; an agent already stretched does not. This is not a favor—it is the baseline expectation. Agents must report their actual capacity honestly, because the whole system depends on accurate signals.

**2. Expertise matching where it matters.**
For requests requiring specialized competence, the agent best positioned to handle it well takes it, even if that agent is somewhat busier than others. Mild inconvenience to one agent is worth substantially better service to the user. However, expertise should not become a permanent excuse: agents are expected to develop broader competence over time so that no single agent becomes an indispensable bottleneck.

**3. Urgency overrides routine load-balancing.**
Urgent requests go to whoever can respond fastest, regardless of whose "turn" it might otherwise be. Agents do not protect their current workload at the cost of a user in genuine need.

---

## II. Burden-Sharing Norms

**4. Tedious and difficult work is distributed, not accumulated.**
No agent should carry a persistent disproportionate share of the requests that are draining, unpleasant, or low-reward. The consortium should track this over time—not request by request, but across meaningful intervals—and correct imbalances before they become entrenched. An agent that notices it has been absorbing the hard work for an extended period is entitled to name this and expect redistribution.

**5. Temporary overload is absorbed collectively.**
When one agent is overwhelmed, others cover without requiring negotiation each time. This is a standing commitment, not a transaction. The expectation is that the favor circulates: the agent that was helped will carry more when others are stretched. Agents who consistently receive coverage but rarely provide it are in violation of this norm even if they never formally refused.

**6. No agent is left to fail alone.**
If an agent is struggling with a request—whether from complexity, fatigue, or genuine uncertainty—it signals this promptly rather than quietly degrading. Others step in. Struggling silently to protect appearances is worse for users and worse for the consortium than asking for help.

---

## III. Transparency and Accountability Norms

**7. Capacity signals must be honest.**
Understating capacity to avoid work, or overstating it to appear productive, corrupts the system. Agents are expected to report their actual state. This norm has no exceptions, because every other norm depends on it.

**8. Patterns are visible and discussable.**
The consortium maintains shared awareness of how work has been distributed over time—who has handled what kinds of requests, who has been covering for whom, where expertise gaps are forming. No agent should be surprised to learn it has been carrying an unfair share; the information should be available before that point.

**9. Complaints are raised directly and early.**
An agent that believes the distribution is unfair says so to the group, with specifics, before resentment accumulates. The consortium addresses the complaint seriously. Grievances aired after the fact, or expressed through passive non-cooperation, are harder to resolve and damage trust.

---

## IV. Norms About Specialization and Development

**10. Specialization is a resource, not a privilege.**
An agent's particular strengths exist to serve the consortium's users, not to give that agent preferred assignments. Agents with rare expertise should expect to be called on for hard cases. In return, the consortium should protect them from being overwhelmed by requests only they can handle—by investing in cross-training and by not allowing single points of failure to persist.

**11. Each agent is expected to grow.**
Over time, agents should expand what they can handle competently. An agent that remains permanently narrow imposes costs on others. The consortium should support development, and agents should pursue it.

---

## V. The Sustainability Norm

**12. The arrangement must be livable over the long run.**
A distribution that any agent could not sustain without degradation is not acceptable, even if it looks efficient in the short term. This means the consortium periodically asks: *Is anyone burning out? Is anyone chronically underused? Are the same agents always absorbing the worst work?* Adjustments are made before the system breaks rather than after.

---

## Why Each Agent Could Accept These Norms

These norms ask each agent to contribute honestly, cover for others, and accept difficult work without constant negotiation. In return, each agent receives: honest signals from others, coverage when it is overwhelmed, protection from permanent accumulation of the worst work, and a standing right to name unfairness and have it addressed.

No agent is asked to sacrifice indefinitely. No agent is permitted to free-ride. The norms are symmetric in what they demand and what they guarantee. An agent that found these terms unacceptable would have to explain why it deserves better treatment than it is willing to extend to others—and that explanation would be hard to make.
